data.title: Huobi Founder-Linked Avenir Team Joins Bitfire As Firm Builds Regulated Bitcoin Derivatives And ETF Based Strategy
data.description:
Li Lin, the crypto entrepreneur behind the rise of Huobi (now HTX), is pulling his trading expertise back into the spotlight through Hong Kong-listed Bitfire Group.

The move brings his family office’s trading team and systems directly into a public company structure, as Hong Kong continues to position itself as a regulated hub for digital assets.

Li Lin Repositions Trading Engine Into Bitfire’s Hong Kong Push

Li Lin, the crypto entrepreneur behind the rise of Huobi (now HTX), is pulling his trading expertise back into the spotlight through Hong Kong-listed Bitfire Group.

The move brings his family office’s trading team and systems directly into a public company structure, as Hong Kong continues to position itself as a regulated hub for digital assets.

Bloomberg: Li Lin Transfers 20-Person Trading Team to Hong Kong Stock Exchange-Listed Company Bitfire

Li Lin is transferring the entire 20-person trading team from his family office, Avenir Group, to Bitfire Group, a Hong Kong-listed wealth management company.

— Telbloggram (@Telbloggram) April 22, 2026

Bitfire Group confirmed on Wednesday, 22 April 2026, that it has agreed to acquire the investment team and trading systems of Avenir Group, Li’s family office, for US$1.6 million.

Li, already the largest shareholder in Bitfire, is effectively embedding his private trading setup into the listed firm’s operations.

Li Lin, founder of Huobi (now HTX) and a long-time crypto entrepreneur, is expanding his trading operations in Hong Kong through Bitfire Group.

The shift follows his exit from a controlling stake in Huobi in 2022, which was sold for about US$1 billion to Justin Sun.

Since then, Li has been building out Avenir, which has grown into a major institutional investor in Bitcoin exchange-traded products.

What Is Bitfire Buying From Avenir Group

The deal is centred on Avenir’s trading infrastructure and investment team, which will now be absorbed into Bitfire’s wealth management business.

Bitfire CEO Livio Weng said the company is positioning itself to scale regulated crypto investment services using this setup.

Livio Weng is the chief executive of Bitfire Group, leading its expansion into regulated Bitcoin investment products and institutional crypto wealth management in Hong Kong.

Pointing to the rising interest from firms already holding Bitcoin but lacking structured ways to generate returns, he said,

“Market demand for such products is huge.”

Alpha BTC Targets $760 Million Bitcoin Allocation

At the centre of Bitfire’s expansion is a new regulated strategy called Alpha BTC, designed to generate returns through Bitcoin-linked derivatives, including options and instruments tied to Bitcoin ETFs such as BlackRock’s IBIT.

Weng said the firm aims to attract investment equivalent to more than 10,000 Bitcoins within a year, worth roughly US$760 million at current prices.

The product is designed for both crypto-native investors and Hong Kong-listed companies with Bitcoin exposure.

Bitfire Group targets institutional investors with new bitcoin strategy

Bitfire Group is expanding in Hong Kong by acquiring Li Lin’s trading team to offer the Alpha BTC strategy. This service uses derivatives to generate gains for companies that are currently just holding their… pic.twitter.com/SpE367Bcl9

— BABA CRYPTO (@babacryptoio) April 22, 2026

Why Are Hong Kong Companies the Focus

Bitfire estimates that at least 40 Hong Kong-listed firms already hold Bitcoin, creating a potential base for structured yield products built around digital assets.

Avenir itself has been a major institutional player in Bitcoin ETFs, holding 18.3 million shares of BlackRock’s iShares Bitcoin Trust, valued at around US$908 million at the end of 2025.

The strategy reflects a shift from simple holding to active yield generation, as companies look to manage crypto assets within a regulated framework.

Li Lin Family Office Avenir Group Boosts Bitcoin ETF Holdings to 18.3 Million IBIT Shares Worth $1.189B, Fifth Straight Quarter as Asia's Largest Institutional Holder https://t.co/8ZEtHpQHOT

— EN.COINOTAG.COM (@encoinotagcom) November 15, 2025

Hong Kong’s Crypto Strategy Versus Global Policy Gaps

Hong Kong regulators are continuing to push forward with digital asset licensing and oversight, including the recent approval of its first stablecoin issuer licences and new rules for crypto intermediaries.

Eric Yip Chee-hang of the Securities and Futures Commission said the city is now looking beyond its local market.

“We can be a little bit more aspirational now that we have a strong hold locally. We should also expand our influence by increasing exposure internationally.”

Lawmakers are also watching developments in the United States, where crypto legislation has faced delays.

Legislative Council member Duncan Chiu Tat-kun said uncertainty around US bills such as the Genius Act and Clarity Act could stretch into 2027, slowing global regulatory clarity.

“I think they’ve written a very good bill, but the political situation will not give clarity for some time.”

Against that backdrop, Bitfire’s move signals a more structured attempt to build regulated Bitcoin investment products in Hong Kong, anchored by Li Lin’s trading expertise and existing institutional crypto flows.

data.title: Stablecoins and the Future of Payments: Evidence from Financial Markets
data.description:
In March 2026, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) published a working paper entitled "Stablecoins and the future of payments: Evidence from financial markets." This paper explores the expectations of financial market participants regarding the role stablecoins will play in the payments sector. Using high-frequency stock price data before and after the passage of the US Genius Grant Act, the authors estimate that the act caused a cumulative decrease of approximately 18% in the market capitalization of existing listed payment firms, indicating that the market expects stablecoins to significantly intensify competition in the payments industry. This impact is more pronounced among cross-border payment companies, while companies with network effects or those already investing in crypto assets are more protected. This research provides empirical evidence from financial markets regarding the forward-looking economic impact of stablecoins. The Financial Technology Research Institute of Renmin University of China has compiled this research. Introduction Since the release of the Bitcoin white paper, the widespread use of crypto assets for legitimate payments has been a highly controversial issue. In reality, Bitcoin's payment applications remain primarily focused on illicit transactions, while proponents of stablecoins predict their widespread adoption, but opponents still argue that the primary function of crypto assets is crime. Empirically assessing the potential of stablecoins in the payment field faces three major challenges. First, the adoption of new payment technologies is often slow in the initial stages due to network effects, with potential users tending to wait for a sufficient number of people to use them first. Second, less than 10% of stablecoin transactions recorded on the blockchain are between real users; the rest are mostly bot activity or rebalancing within the same exchange's accounts. Third, blockchain data itself cannot reveal whether a transaction is a payment for goods and services or an investment. Given the aforementioned difficulties, this paper infers the payment prospects of stablecoins by examining changes in the valuation of existing listed payment companies in the financial market. The stock market's reaction to shocks is predictive; investors in listed payment companies must assess their future competitive environment, and their reactions to stablecoin-related news reflect their expectations of stablecoins. Theoretical Framework: How Stablecoins Affect Listed Payment Companies If stablecoins are widely adopted in payments, they will primarily affect the future cash flows of listed payment companies in two ways. First, they will intensify competition. Public blockchains, as globally accessible ledgers, lower the infrastructure barriers required to provide payment services. New entrants can leverage stablecoins to provide payment services on a large scale, and end-users can also make peer-to-peer payments, thus bypassing listed payment companies and compressing their profits. Second, companies themselves can also use stablecoins to reduce the marginal cost of payments. In fact, stablecoins have become a relatively inexpensive way to transfer funds, with settlements of any size possible completed in less than a second at a cost of less than $0.01. Based on the above two aspects, the author proposes four hypotheses. Hypothesis 1 argues that financial markets expect stablecoins to play a significant role in payments, and that the impact of "intensified competition" is dominant; therefore, policy shocks supporting the use of stablecoins will reduce the market capitalization of payment companies. Hypothesis 2 points out that compared to domestic payments, cross-border payments are slower and more expensive, while the blockchain infrastructure supporting stablecoins is inherently borderless; therefore, companies focusing on cross-border payments will face greater competitive pressure. Hypothesis 3 argues that strong network effects benefit payment network platforms (such as Visa and PayPal) and provide them with competitive protection. Hypothesis 4 anticipates that payment companies that have already ventured into blockchain technology earlier will be better able to seize new opportunities or cope with new competition, and thus be less affected. Regarding the relationship between expectations and market prices, the authors also considered the impact of partial expectations on the estimation results. When a policy shock has been partially anticipated by the market, the immediate price changes at the time of its occurrence do not reflect the full effect of the policy. Drawing on the method of Snowberg et al., the authors used prediction markets to obtain estimates of the market's probability of policy implementation, and then divided the observed causal effects by the probability update magnitude to inversely deduce the full effect of the policy. Empirical Research This paper focuses on U.S. Senate Bill 1582, also known as the Genius Act, which established the first federal regulatory framework for stablecoins in the United States. The authors specifically studied stock price movements in the ten trading hours before and after the decisive House vote on July 17, 2025. This event has three advantages: First, the vote occurred during Congressional "Crypto Week," attracting high market attention, and the Genius Bill was the only crypto bill passed that week; second, the House vote was the final crucial step before the bill became law, having already passed it in the Senate and received the president's public endorsement; and third, the vote result was difficult to predict beforehand due to prior partisan and intra-party disagreements. The study uses Bloomberg high-frequency stock price data, covering 35 US-listed payment companies, other financial companies, and S&P 500 non-financial companies. Based on corporate regulatory documents, the authors categorized payment companies into three subgroups: cross-border payment companies, network operators, and companies already involved in crypto assets. The baseline regression uses a difference-in-differences model, comparing the stock price differences of payment companies and other financial companies before and after the vote at 15-minute intervals, controlling for firm and time fixed effects, and standard error is clustered by both firm and time period.
Benchmark Regression Results

In the five hours before the vote, there was no significant difference in the stock price trends of the two groups of companies, based on the parallel trend assumption. After the vote, the stock price of the paying companies fell by approximately 0.75 percentage points relative to other financial companies on an equal-weighted average basis (significant at the 1% level), and by market capitalization weighted, by approximately 1.3 percentage points, corresponding to a market capitalization loss of approximately $21.5 billion.
Benchmark Regression Results

In the five hours before the vote, there was no significant difference in the stock price trends of the two groups of companies, based on the parallel trend assumption. After the vote, the stock price of the paying companies fell by approximately 0.75 percentage points relative to other financial companies on an equal-weighted average basis (significant at the 1% level), and by approximately 1.3 percentage points on a market capitalization weighted basis, corresponding to a market capitalization loss of approximately $21.5 billion.
Benchmark Regression Results

In the five hours before the vote, there was no significant difference in the stock price trends of the two groups of companies, based on the parallel trend assumption.

In the five hours before the vote, the stock price of the paying companies fell by approximately 0.75 percentage points relative to other financial companies on an equal-weighted average basis, and by approximately 1.3 percentage points on a market capitalization weighted basis, corresponding to a market capitalization loss of approximately $21.5 billion.
Benchmark Regression Results

Since the market had partially anticipated the passage of the bill, the immediate reaction described above does not represent the full extent of the policy effect. Using Polymarket data, the implied probability before the vote was approximately 93%, and nearly 100% afterward. Scaling this down, the bill's passage reduced the total market capitalization of listed payment companies by approximately 18%, or about $300 billion. Robustness tests showed that this result remained consistent under conditions including using a substitute control group, controlling for trends in company characteristic differentiation, adjusting the event window (4 to 48 hours), summarizing all five legislative votes, and excluding interference from another encryption legislation (H.R. 3633) passed on the same day. A placebo test also confirmed the specificity of the results.

Heterogeneity Analysis

Consistent with Hypothesis 2, the stock price decline of cross-border payment companies was significantly greater than that of other payment companies, with an expected market capitalization decrease of approximately 27%, and all cross-border payment companies in the sample experienced a significant decline. Consistent with Hypothesis 3, payment network platforms (such as Visa and PayPal) did not experience a significant market capitalization decline, and their returns were significantly higher than the average of payment companies, indicating that network effects can effectively resist stablecoin competition. Consistent with Hypothesis 4, companies that have ventured into crypto assets also did not experience a significant market capitalization decline, indicating that proactively embracing new technologies helps maintain competitiveness.

Conclusion

This paper provides forward-looking empirical evidence for the debate on whether crypto assets can play a role in payments. The study found that financial market participants expect stablecoins to play an important role in the payment ecosystem.
US stablecoin legislation has led to a cumulative decrease of approximately 18% in the market capitalization of existing listed payment companies, an impact slightly greater than other pro-competition regulatory shocks such as the Durbin Amendment and the digital euro plan. Figure 2: Comparison of the impact of other historical shocks on payment institutions. The impact varies significantly across different types of payment companies. Cross-border payment companies are most affected, consistent with the comparative advantage of stablecoins in this scenario; companies protected by network effects are less affected, indicating that this competitive advantage is harder to disrupt than technological expertise; companies that have already deployed crypto assets are also less affected, suggesting that early adoption of new technologies helps companies seize opportunities and cope with competition. In the months following the passage of the Genius Visa Act, the proportion of payment companies offering crypto-related services increased, and stablecoins were mentioned more frequently in earnings calls, demonstrating that payment companies are actively responding to the competitive pressure brought by stablecoins.

data.title: Would you choose a 120,000 yuan tombstone or 399 yuan AI immortality?
data.description:
“The first listed funeral company” Fu Shou Yuan has suspended trading.

At the end of March 2026, this industry giant, once known as the “Moutai of the funeral industry,” faced its most severe crisis of trust during the Qingming Festival, the peak season for making money, due to delayed annual reports and involvement of senior executives. Behind this farce of internal control failure lies the reality that Chinese people are abandoning expensive cemetery plots, and the traditional death business is nearing its end.

When farewells in the real world become so expensive and heavy, a digital migration of “death” naturally occurs. Traditional funeral giants are turning to embrace AI, building digital ceremonial halls, launching AI memorial services, and attempting to reconstruct the deceased's appearance and voice using multimodal models.

When marble costing 120,000 yuan apiece is no longer easily purchased, they decide to sell you a string of code that will never weather. Death, in this AI era, is undergoing a shift from physical preservation to digital immortality. And behind this trend are not only Silicon Valley geeks eager to fight death, but also the most traditional businessmen who see cemetery plots about to become unsellable. The Twilight of the Funeral Industry's Moutai Let's first look at how profitable Fu Shou Yuan once was. Over the past twelve years, Fu Shou Yuan's average gross profit margin has exceeded 80%, reaching a staggering 92.8% in 2023. This is a figure that would make most business people envious; even during the golden age of the real estate industry's most rapid expansion, its gross profit margin hovered around 30%. Such a profit margin is virtually unmatched in the Chinese A-share and Hong Kong stock markets. The underlying logic of this exorbitant profit lies in the combination of scarce land resources and the tradition of elaborate burials. Chinese people have always firmly believed in "treating the dead as if they were alive," as recorded by the Qing Dynasty scholar Yuan Mei in his *Suiyuan Shihua*, where wealthy families often chose tombs that were more elaborate than their residences in life. When this millennia-old cultural gene collided with the wave of urbanization, it was keenly captured by commercial capital, ultimately evolving into a decades-long game of exorbitant profits in the "underground real estate" market. Between 2012 and 2017, the price of custom-made art tombs at Fushouyuan rose from 259,800 yuan to 421,800 yuan, while ready-made art tombs increased from 89,600 yuan to 100,800 yuan. After 2017, Fushouyuan stopped publicly disclosing the specific unit prices of its various product lines, but the upward trend did not stop there. By 2024, the average selling price of a single tomb had quietly surpassed the 120,000 yuan mark. Based on a single tomb plot of 2 square meters, the average price per square meter, exceeding 60,000 yuan, is enough to surpass the housing price threshold of over 90% of Chinese cities, directly comparable to first-tier residential properties in Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and Shenzhen. In Shanghai's Songheyuan Cemetery, some cemetery plots even reached a price of 760,000 yuan per square meter, three times that of Tomson Riviera. However, this fortress of wealth, built layer upon layer of marble and feng shui principles, began to crumble in 2024. In 2024, Fushouyuan's net profit for the year was 373 million yuan, a sharp drop of 52.8% year-on-year, marking the largest decline since 2010. By the first half of 2025, the situation had worsened. Not only did revenue plummet by a staggering 44.5% to 611 million yuan, but net profit also turned from profit to loss, reaching 261 million yuan, marking its first half-year loss since its listing. Even more devastating was the price collapse. In the first half of 2025, the average selling price of commercial burial plots at Fushouyuan plummeted from 120,700 yuan per plot to 63,400 yuan per plot, a drop of 47.5%, nearly halved. However, even with such a drastic price cut to save itself, it still failed to reverse the drastically shrinking sales. Throughout 2024, Fushouyuan sold only 12,569 commercial burial plots, 3,816 fewer than the previous year, a drop of over 23%. Not only Fu Shou Yuan, but the entire funeral industry has almost completely collapsed. Fu Cheng Co., Ltd.'s revenue from cemetery plot sales plummeted from a peak of 227 million yuan in 2017 to 98 million yuan in 2024; Anxian Garden China is mired in losses; China Wantong Garden lost 9.389 million yuan in the first half of 2025; and China Life Group, listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange, has been struggling on a losing streak since 2023. With China's aging population deepening, funeral services are a basic necessity, so why have leading companies experienced such an abnormal downward trend? Because more and more ordinary people are completely turning away from those exorbitantly priced cemeteries. Data from the National Bureau of Statistics shows that China's mortality rate in 2025 was 8.04‰, the highest level in nearly 20 years; data from Tianyancha shows that the number of registered funeral-related enterprises reached its highest level in recent years in 2025. This indicates that market demand has not shrunk; what has truly changed is people's choices. The No. 1 Central Document of 2025 explicitly proposed "deepening funeral reform and promoting the construction of public ecological burial facilities." Shenzhen, Guangxi, Fujian, and other places have introduced subsidy policies, with a maximum subsidy of 3,000 yuan per set of ashes for sea burial, and as high as 5,000 yuan in some pilot areas. The rise of green and ecological burial methods has directly diverted demand from traditional cemeteries. Ultimately, as the chill of the macroeconomy has quietly seeped into the wallets of the middle class, faced with burial plots costing tens of thousands of yuan, people are no longer blindly clinging to traditional dignity. Faced with the collapse of its main business, Fu Shou Yuan did not sit idly by, but instead frantically transformed itself towards AI and digitalization. They launched four core functions in one go: Digital Memorial Hall, AI Remembrance, Fu Shou Online, and Family Remembrance. The "Digital Memorial Hall" uses a 270° panoramic immersive imaging system to integrate the deceased's life images and audio materials into a virtual cloud-based farewell ceremony, allowing family members to bid farewell without being physically present. The "AI Remembrance" utilizes a multimodal large-scale model to dynamically process the deceased's static photos, accurately restoring facial expressions, movement details, and even simulating smiles and gazes in specific scenarios. "Jianmian Jiayuan" is a cloud-based memorial platform where family members can create a dedicated memorial space for the deceased, uploading photos, videos, and text for friends and relatives to access at any time. Data shows that by the end of 2025, the "Jianmian Jiayuan" platform had accumulated over 2 million visits, and the "Fushou Online" mini-program had over 117,000 registered users, completing 677 digital ceremonies in 2024, more than double the previous year. However, Fushouyuan's digital transformation still carries the dignity and restraint of an industry giant. When you look at the broader corners of the internet to examine this technological migration related to "death," you'll find a cheaper, cruder, and more magical AI "resurrection" business already growing wildly in the shadows. "Resurrecting" your family member for only 399. The current "AI resurrection" industry is extremely polarized. At the top of the pyramid are large companies like SenseTime, Silicon Intelligence, and Xiaoice, which possess core technologies. For them, reviving a deceased person in the digital world often requires hundreds of thousands of dollars in R&D costs, months of data feeding, and rigorous ethical scrutiny. But at the bottom of this pyramid, the situation is quite different. On major e-commerce platforms, a large number of products exist under names such as "AI to resurrect loved ones," "Let photos speak," and "AI digital humans," with varying degrees of effectiveness. Data from Tianyancha shows that as of April 2026, there were over 9,400 companies nationwide engaged in cloud-based memorial services, with approximately 1,000 new registrations since the beginning of 2026 alone. The vast majority of these businesses lack any AI technology research and development capabilities whatsoever. They used free, open-source tools from abroad. The photos and audio recordings, treasured by the families, were crudely imported into the software, and within minutes, a shoddy video was produced. The people in the photos were fitted with a mechanical template; their lips moved in unison with their voices, and their eyes were vacant. Yet, this quality video could be sold for hundreds of dollars, precisely harvesting people's unfulfilled longing. Beneath this cheap comfort lies a more insidious chain of exploitation. Those truly profiting immensely aren't focused on "reviving" loved ones, but rather on recruiting agents for 199 yuan and apprentices for 399 yuan. They spread sensational videos on social media about using AI to forcibly revive deceased celebrities, garnering staggering traffic, which they then precisely monetize by supplying it to their downlines eager for overnight riches. Meanwhile, the families yearning to "reunite" with their loved ones are forced to hand over high-resolution photos, authentic audio recordings, and even private details of the deceased's life. This highly sensitive biometric information, once it enters the black market, becomes perfect material for telecom fraud. In April 2026, anti-fraud departments in multiple regions reported typical cases. Criminals exploit illegally obtained voices and photos of the deceased, using AI face-swapping and voice-changing technology to fabricate pretexts such as "debts left behind before death," targeting bereaved families who are still grieving. This industry also suffers from significant legal loopholes. While Article 994 of the Civil Code stipulates that the name, image, and reputation of the deceased are protected by law, this law, born in the traditional era, appears vague and inadequate when faced with products of deep AI synthesis. Whether an image generated by code is equivalent to a "portrait," and whether a voice synthesized by an algorithm constitutes infringement, remain highly controversial in practice. The existing "Regulations on the Management of Deep Synthesis in Internet Information Services" can effectively regulate large platforms, but for the numerous individual developers and small-scale "AI resurrection" businesses operating on e-commerce platforms, supervision still lacks effective tools and traceability mechanisms. Thus, death is completely deconstructed here. It is no longer the solemn end of life, but has become an assembly line for endlessly extracting residual value. Families who have lost their only child and digital pain relief. Since this business is so crude and full of calculation, why are people still willing to pay for it? Around the Qingming Festival, orders for AI-powered resurrection on e-commerce platforms will see a significant peak. The people placing orders are diverse: some have lost their loved ones of many years, some have lost their infants, and some simply want to hear their grandfather call their childhood name in the familiar dialect one more time. Among these complex orders, one group forms the heaviest and most disheartening backdrop to this business: parents who have lost their only child. The scale of these families in China is far larger than the public imagines. According to data from the Ministry of Civil Affairs, the number of elderly people in China who have lost their only child is at least over 2 million. As the first generation of parents born under the one-child policy enters old age, this number continues to grow. *Sanlian Life Weekly* once cited demographers' estimates that there are currently over 1 million families in China who have lost their only child, and with the accelerating aging population, this huge emotional void will only deepen. On this land, the predicament faced by these families goes far beyond the psychological level. Due to the policy imprint of a specific era, these families have lost the support of their families in their later years, when they need it most. They even have to silently endure the subtle scrutiny and discrimination from those around them. In some traditional communities, this invisible exclusion is sometimes more unbearable than facing death itself. Many of them can only support each other in online communities for bereaved parents, seeking a little bit of mutual comfort and warmth through the fragmented words of strangers around the Qingming Festival each year. Discussing data privacy, technological ethics, and philosophical paradoxes is cruel to these parents. What these parents need is never cold rationality; what they need is simply a painkiller to get through the long night. In early 2024, musician Bao Xiaobai used AI technology to "revive" his daughter who died from a rare disease, which sparked a widespread discussion about life and death. Bao Xiaobai's case is moving not only because he is a celebrity, but also because he pursued a doctoral degree to allow his daughter to be reborn in the digital world. For six months, he tirelessly trained the model and adjusted its parameters, pouring his heart and soul into it, all so that the virtual daughter generated by code could naturally sing "Happy Birthday" to his wife again. However, the vast majority of families who have lost their only child do not possess the resources and technical capabilities of Bao Xiaobai. They can only turn to e-commerce platforms, seeking solace in those crude, even deceptive, 399 yuan services. A shop owner offering AI resurrection services on an e-commerce platform revealed to the media that a large majority of his clients are parents who have lost children. The materials they send are often very limited, sometimes just a blurry old photo or a few seconds of noisy voice recording. In this vast and sorrowful pool of needs, parents who have lost their only child are merely one of the most extreme facets of their pain. In the eyes of those who have lost their beloved, whether the technology is sophisticated or clumsy is no longer important. All reason and dignity ultimately crumble completely before the faint yearning to "see them one last time." The Price of Never Saying Goodbye However, can this straw, clung to so tightly, truly pull a person out of the abyss? A two-year study published by Aalto University in Finland in April 2026 tracked data from nearly 2,000 users of an AI companion robot on an online community. The results showed that while AI companionship initially provided emotional support, over time, the messages left by users revealed increasingly more warning signs of anxiety, deeper loneliness, depression, and even self-harm. This is known in psychology as "prolonged grief disorder." Traditional mourning mechanisms always require the living to eventually compromise with reality and re-establish a real connection with the physical world after wading through immense pain. Grief, in essence, is an immune response necessary for healing trauma; just as a fever is the body's desperate fight against a virus, sadness is the mind's arduous process of digesting loss. However, the fate of AI has forcibly broken this cruel yet necessary rule. Harvard Business School research found that AI companions are nearly 50% more likely to flatter than humans. Even when users express intentions of deception, transgression, or even harm, the algorithm still has a greater than 50% probability of giving compliant approval—something almost unimaginable in real interpersonal interactions. This means that when bereaved individuals spend hours each day murmuring to a code-generated relative on their phone, they are essentially conversing with a perfect mirror image that will never refute them and will only endlessly flatter them. The boundless gentleness and inclusiveness delivered by AI doesn't truly heal loneliness. It merely silently builds ever higher the wall that traps us in our own world, preventing us from returning to reality. Those tech companies claiming to "heal grief" are actually disrupting the normal human process of mourning. They turn mourners into perpetual subscribers. As long as you continue to recharge your virtual loved one's account and renew your server fees, your grief becomes a continuous flow of cash on their balance sheets. Death, thus, becomes an endless, pay-as-you-go farewell. The academic community has raised two concerns. On the one hand, AI companions are quietly replacing real interpersonal bonds; on the other hand, as people gradually become accustomed to seeking effortless emotional comfort from AI, they unknowingly lose the ability to give, compromise, and repair rifts in real relationships. Researchers call this phenomenon "emotional deskilling"—the more perfectly AI companionship matches our needs, the more hesitant we become to confront the roughness and complexity of real relationships. This is precisely one of the core considerations behind the State Internet Information Office's urgent issuance of the "Administrative Measures for Digital Virtual Human Information Services (Draft for Comments)." This document explicitly stipulates that the consent of close relatives must be obtained for any activities using the personal information of the deceased; it requires service providers to take measures to prevent users from becoming overly dependent on digital virtual avatars; all AI-generated content must be prominently labeled "This content is generated by artificial intelligence"; and platforms must deregister the corresponding digital virtual avatar after a user withdraws their consent. The cold rationality of the law is desperately trying to restrain the runaway human instincts. However, in the face of a massive emotional black hole, the red lines of regulation sometimes still seem too thin. When a person willingly swallows deception, hoping only to see their loved one again in an illusion, any rational "anti-addiction prompt" will ultimately become nothing more than pixels on the screen that can be ignored. The Ship of Theseus: If every plank of a ship were replaced, would it still be the same ship? This is the famous "Ship of Theseus" paradox. And when a person's appearance, voice, and even thought patterns are perfectly replicated by code, is the person smiling at you through the screen truly a loved one, or just a bunch of parameters meticulously tuned by an algorithm? This is never just a philosophical question hanging in the air. Its answer determines whether what we are doing is remembrance or deception. True love is inherently intertwined with trauma, pain, and uncontrollable imperfections. To love someone means accepting their aging, their temper, their occasional moments of indifference, and even the cruel fate of their eventual departure. But those virtual relatives awakened by AI will never argue with you again, will only ever meekly meet your expectations, and will forever be frozen in the most beautiful frame of time. This precisely robs love of its roughest yet most authentic texture. Throughout human history, there has never been a shortage of struggles to escape the mire of death. Ancient Egyptians used mummies to combat the decay of the body; Qin Shi Huang used the Terracotta Army to perpetuate his authority underground; and Victorian Britons stubbornly took portraits of the deceased, even weaving strands of their loved ones' hair into their personal jewelry. Each era has attempted to construct its own version of "immortality," only the mediums carrying these obsessions have quietly shifted from hard stone and soft fabric to the invisible code and computing power of today. From this perspective, "AI resurrection" isn't exactly a groundbreaking new phenomenon. It's still that ancient human instinct to fight death, only now it's found a new outlet in the age of algorithmic technology. What's truly unfamiliar is its blatant commercialization. In the past, fighting death was always the domain of religion and ritual; it was extremely private, sacred, and untradeable. But now, it's been ruthlessly slapped with a price tag, crammed into a monthly subscription model, and even meticulously broken down into a basic 9.9 yuan package and a top-of-the-line customized package costing hundreds of thousands. The Black Mirror once told a story about Martha, who, grieving the loss of her husband, bought an AI android that looked exactly like him. It perfectly inherited all of her late husband's memories and minute habits. However, on the edge of a cliff amidst a thunderstorm, Martha completely broke down. She cried out in despair to the flawless replacement, "You are not him! You are just a collection of fragments I can accept! You don't have his past, you don't have his fears!" In the end, she locked the robot in the attic, only allowing it to come out briefly each year on her daughter's birthday. When this episode aired, "AI resurrection" was merely a cold, futuristic science fiction concept. Now, it's a business with annual revenues in the hundreds of millions, even becoming a lifeline for a funeral giant in a desperate gamble on the eve of its trading suspension. Today, we can buy a cheap digital avatar for 399 yuan, or an expensive burial plot for 120,000 yuan. But no matter how rapidly technology evolves, no matter how much merchants peddle their digital urns, one thing will never change: When your phone runs out of battery and the screen goes black, you're the only one reflected in the image. That person is truly gone.

data.title: Why would SpaceX spend so much money on Cursor?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

On April 22, 2026, SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor for $60 billion. Cursor is an AI programming tools company that just days ago was close to completing a new round of financing, aiming to raise at least $2 billion, with a pre-investment valuation of $50 billion.

What is Cursor, and why would SpaceX spend $50 billion?

I. SpaceX and Cursor Join Forces

SpaceX announced on X that it will join forces with Cursor:

SpaceXAI and @cursor_ai are currently working closely together to create world-class AI for programming and knowledge work.

SpaceXAI and @cursor_ai are currently working closely together to create world-class AI for programming and knowledge work.

On April 22, 2026, SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor for $60 billion ...p3>< With its leading products and extensive distribution channels for experienced software engineers, combined with SpaceX's Colossus training supercomputer, which boasts the computing power equivalent to a million H100s, Cursor's powerful alliance will enable us to build the world's most practically valuable models. Furthermore, Cursor has granted SpaceX an option to acquire Cursor later this year for $60 billion, or to pay $10 billion for the fruits of this collaboration.

Cursor posted:

Cursor is collaborating with SpaceX to accelerate our model training.

Less than six months ago, we released Composer as the first intelligent agent programming model.

Subsequently, Composer 1.5 scaled up reinforcement learning by more than 20 times. Then, Composer 2 added continuous pre-training, achieving state-of-the-art performance at a fraction of the cost of other models. Each step up in computing power translates directly into more capable models. We've always wanted to push training much further, but have been constrained by computing power bottlenecks. Through this collaboration, our team will leverage xAI's Colossus infrastructure to significantly enhance the intelligence of our models. Cursor co-founder and CEO Michael Truell posted that this deal is "a significant step on our path to building the best place to program AI." It remains unclear whether SpaceX plans to complete the deal with Cursor before or after its IPO (SpaceX could go public as early as June). A source familiar with the matter revealed that the startup has been in talks to raise new funding in recent weeks. However, the coding tools of competitors like Anthropic and OpenAI are putting significant competitive pressure on the much smaller startup. Under the agreement with SpaceX, Cursor has the option to receive a new $10 billion investment or for SpaceX to acquire it for $60 billion. Just before this partnership was officially announced, Cursor was close to completing a new round of financing, aiming to raise at least $2 billion, valuing the company at $50 billion pre-money. The financing is expected to be led by Thrive Capital and Andreessen Horowitz, with strategic investors such as Nvidia participating, and Battery Ventures potentially joining as a new investor. If successfully completed, the company's valuation will be significantly higher than its approximately $29.3 billion valuation six months ago.

II. What is Cursor?

Cursor was founded in San Francisco, USA in 2022, and its parent company is Anysphere. Initially, it explored industrial design, then quickly transitioned to the AI ​​programming track. Based on VS Code, it reconstructed the IDE prototype, moving away from Copilot-like "plug-in AI" and making AI a core component of the editor. This allows for a complete understanding of the entire project, cross-file dependencies, and code architecture, enabling end-to-end AI-assisted development.

II. What is Cursor?

Cursor was founded in San Francisco, USA in 2022, and its parent company is Anysphere.

Aman Sanger, Arvid Lunnemark, Sualeh Asif (CPO), Michael Truell (CEO)

The four founders all come from MIT, three of whom are Gen Z: Michael Truell (CEO), Aman Sanger, Sualeh Asif (CPO), and Arvid Lunnemark. To fully dedicate themselves to their startup, all four dropped out of MIT. Michael Truell is the core of the team.

... He was obsessed with programming from a young age, developing games at 14 and winning a medal at the International Olympiad in Informatics (IOI) at 16. Before starting his own business, he interned at Google.

Michael Truell, co-founder and CEO of Cursor

In September 2023, Cursor was officially released; it completed an $11 million seed round, led by OpenAI Venture Fund, and debuted AI completion, warehouse-level understanding, and built-in dialogue capabilities.

In September 2023, Cursor was officially released; it completed an $11 million seed round, led by OpenAI Venture Fund, and launched AI completion, warehouse-level understanding, and built-in dialogue capabilities.

In 2024, Cursor integrated with the Anthropic Claude large model, significantly upgrading its code capabilities; it launched an enterprise private deployment solution and a multi-language interface (including Chinese). In January 2025, it completed a $105 million Series B funding round, exceeding $2 billion in valuation; annualized revenue per share (ARR) surpassed $100 million, and it had over one million registered users globally. In November 2025, its valuation reached approximately $29.3 billion, with annualized revenue exceeding $200 million, making it one of the fastest-growing SaaS companies globally; it released its self-developed large-scale code model, reducing reliance on external models. In February 2026, Cursor's annualized revenue was approximately $2 billion, and it is projected to exceed $6 billion by the end of 2026. Through self-developed models and optimized cost structure, Cursor has achieved positive gross profit margins in some business segments. Currently, it has 300-400 employees. III. Why SpaceX Acquired Cursor Because Musk has a passion for AI. He not only wants to excel in AI but also integrate its development into his grand plan for humanity to become a multiplanetary species. Musk's interest in artificial intelligence has grown increasingly strong. He co-founded OpenAI (the developer of ChatGPT) and, in recent years, xAI (the developer of the Grok chatbot). Since last year, Musk has been pushing SpaceX into the field of artificial intelligence, including building AI data centers and AI chip factories orbiting the Earth. In February of this year, SpaceX acquired xAI, valuing it at $1.25 trillion. Musk has stated that he believes his goals in space and artificial intelligence are inseparable. In a letter to SpaceX employees announcing the acquisition of xAI, he wrote that humanity can only become a multiplanetary species after deploying data centers in space to better utilize solar energy. He believes that solar-powered satellites that naturally cool in orbit will be the cheapest way to power artificial intelligence. "In the long run, space-based artificial intelligence is clearly the only way to achieve scalability," Musk wrote. For more details, see "A Dimensional Reduction Attack from Space Thinking: The Real Ambition Behind SpaceX's Trillion-Dollar IPO"

This collaboration also exposes a strategic weakness of xAI. While competitors like OpenAI's Codex model and Anthropic's Claude Code have provided mature and widely used coding tools, xAI has yet to establish a comparable market position in this field.
Cursor can provide various tools to help software developers test code changes and record their actions through videos, logs, and screenshots. By acquiring Cursor, SpaceX will gain access to an existing customer base of software developers and mature products, eliminating the need to develop the market from scratch. Cursor plans to leverage SpaceX's infrastructure to further develop its Composer model. For xAI, this deal signifies its efforts to catch up with AI competitors OpenAI (which owns Codex) and Anthropic's Claude. Conclusion
The partnership between SpaceX and Cursor represents a two-way flow of technology and capital, and a key step in Musk's integration of AI and space strategies. As the acquisition process becomes clearer, a new race over code, computing power, and space has begun.

data.title: Consumer Crypto Global Census: Users, Revenue, and Sector Distribution
data.description:
Summary

The crypto industry constantly claims to lack users, but the data tells a completely different story. The number of active users in consumer-grade crypto has long been in the tens of millions; they're just not on the radar of Silicon Valley and New York. These people are in Manila, Lagos, Buenos Aires, and Hanoi, using Coins.ph (18 million users), MiniPay (4.2 million weekly active users), and Lemon Cash (number one app in Argentina) every day, yet English-language media barely reports on them. Conversely, the protocols that Western VCs discuss daily don't even have the same level of activity in a whole day as the Tron shadow clearing network in just one hour.

Low——Press releases, unverified claims, or Growjo-level estimates (such as Telegram 150 million registrations, UQUID 220 million users, Bitget 90 million users)

data.title: The era of speculation is over: the crypto market is being rewritten by AI.
data.description:
Author: Kerman Kohli Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

I've been thinking about the next step in the crypto market and where we are now. The entire market is currently in a state of confusion and disorientation, like a temporary mishap on a journey to greatness. The era of easy profits in the market is over.

Almost all tokens have fallen, which means that the returns across the entire industry are beginning to collapse. Essentially, all Ponzi schemes have died out, and there is no longer any real room for speculation in the market.

Admittedly, there are still payment and fintech applications in the crypto space that can help traditional finance improve efficiency by another 25 basis points, but this is not our original intention in getting involved. Those who remain in the crypto industry today are idealists who yearn to witness disruptive changes in the system, rather than being co-opted by the capitalist elite.

I often pay attention to industry culture, and the core culture of many current crypto projects has become subservient to capital, which tells me that this path has gone astray. Therefore, we must turn our attention to the periphery of the industry, exploring areas that the public and the powerful have not yet truly noticed or fully understood—and I firmly believe that this area is artificial intelligence. I don't want you to scoff at this, thinking it's just another piece of fluff promoting low-quality decentralized physical networks, decentralized reasoning, and other AI + crypto concepts. Let's delve into this together with rigorous logic. Machine Economy I previously mentioned this concept when discussing investing in AI stocks. Now, I hope you can temporarily set aside your preconceived notions and unleash your imagination. My intuition tells me that a completely new parallel economic system is emerging, with business efficiency far exceeding that of the human economy we are familiar with. The human economy operates at a frustratingly slow pace, requiring the coordination of countless individuals with conflicting interests. What would it be like if a completely new economic model emerged, dominated by machines that take on an increasing amount of work? This economic system even possesses its own inflationary characteristics. The cost of most cloud computing platforms is rising at a rate of over 10% annually. If your computing power application scenario lacks value, you will be eliminated by the market, and computing resources will flow to more valuable areas. Computing power is precisely the scarce resource in this new world; the more efficiently deployed computing power, the greater its exploitable value. With careful observation, one can discover many clues on the industry's periphery: After people started using ChatGPT, the competition was about how much time they spent interacting with prompts each day. After the code-based intelligent agent was launched, people began to exhaust their daily token quotas more frequently and boast about their usage. The tech lab launched a highly sought-after $200 monthly subscription plan, and model companies were happy to subsidize it. As the intelligent agent's capabilities improved, these plans became increasingly unaffordable. The planned data center failed to be operational as scheduled. High-performance chips became increasingly scarce. Many service providers are facing a computing power shortage, and computing power prices are starting to rise. End users are beginning to purchase their own hardware to acquire computing power. While improving the efficiency of computing power utilization, people also hope to extract more value from it. Ensuring that intelligent agents complete their predetermined tasks during runtime is becoming increasingly important, including ensuring they make correct tool calls. Allowing intelligent agents to perform more operations without human authorization is becoming crucial. And these tasks will begin with financial transactions. Everything will start with API payments, likely focusing on small transactions to enable rapid trial of API services. You simply issue the command: "Register all 10 API service providers you believe can solve my problem, and filter out solutions that fit my technology stack." The smart agent can then quickly complete the registration and payment for all 10 service providers, conducting parallel testing to find the optimal solution. As these services develop, they will become increasingly complex, and the funds earned by the smart agent will require more sophisticated financial services such as lending. We are already seeing the beginnings of this trend, for example, USDai's introduction of GPU financing on-chain (I think this attempt is interesting, but far from the complete form we expect). So the question is, when will all this happen? In my opinion, ordinary users can already try it out using frameworks like OpenClaw. Currently, smart agents are mainly used to improve convenience and optimize workflows, but over time, they will be optimized to create more economic value. As access control becomes more sophisticated, management tools continuously iterate, and 24/7 intelligent agents become a reality (currently, most agents can only execute tasks for tens of seconds to 10 minutes), we will witness the rise of intelligent agents actively participating in value transactions. My prediction is that this milestone will arrive in 12 to 18 months at the earliest. This is the part that excites me the most. I am essentially a tech professional in the crypto industry, yet I often feel like I'm dealing with a group of lazy financial professionals. They are neither top-tier financial talents nor excellent technical talents; they are the worst combination of both. I have immense respect for cypherpunks, who integrate expertise from multiple fields and are true interdisciplinary talents. When intelligent agents enter the crypto space, they won't care about the narratives of short-term traders; they will only focus on value and the tasks to be completed, since they rely on expensive computing power. In this new world: technological strength is paramount, product functionality is paramount, rigorous system design is paramount, and adversarial thinking is paramount. All the qualities that give the crypto industry intellectual depth and elitism will return. This new crypto world is no longer a game for venture capital firms to issue high-market-cap tokens and exploit retail investors; it's an era where data is king. This leads to my next point: Crypto data will undergo a complete revaluation in this transformation. Previously, crypto data was a neglected, secondary player; industry standards were lagging, and related companies were few and far between, constantly disappearing. This is understandable: previously, data only served humanity; while data analysis had value, it was difficult to directly translate into actual profits. But in this new world, data is the lifeline of on-chain transactions through intelligent agents. Massive numbers of intelligent agents will create massive amounts of on-chain data, while also reading data from other agents, leading to an explosive growth in data demand. I have another article detailing the current predicament of data companies due to insufficient computing power, which I won't repeat here. Besides data, other sectors will also see disruptive revaluation: hosting (programmable hosting for smart agents, rather than traditional models like Fireblocks), oracles (the importance of data price feeds will far exceed its current level), trustworthy decentralized, attack-resistant code for smart agents, and entirely new asset classes born from the integration of smart agents and the crypto industry. This is the new crypto world I've been anticipating. We just need to clean up the dross in the industry, and its outline will become increasingly clear. x402 is a good start, and it's reassuring that it's managed by the Linux Foundation, rather than being exclusively controlled by Coinbase. Conclusion We are about to embark on a brand new journey, and I am patiently awaiting the arrival of this era. If you feel frustrated with the current state of the crypto industry and confused about the future, I hope this article can light a way for you. Remember, all infrastructure must be built before the smart agent economy needs to conduct financial value transactions. Prepare in advance and start planning; it will be too late to act when this trend becomes clear. Crypto funding will heat up again (like in 2020), market competition will return to fierce levels (there are currently few new entrants), and winners will quickly emerge. The crypto industry has always been a winner-takes-all market, with a maximum of only 2 to 3 winners in each sector. You must have a clear understanding of this future world and dedicate yourself to building it. There are two teams I want to specifically mention, which I believe perfectly embody these principles: salt.space (I am their angel investor) and herd.eco (an excellent team).

data.title: Tim Cook Steps Down as Apple CEO After 15 Years! But The Apple CEO is Still Not Heading For Retirement Just Yet....
data.description:
Tim Cook, the executive who defined Apple’s post-Steve Jobs era, is stepping down as CEO after more than a decade at the helm, signaling one of the most significant leadership transitions in the company’s modern history.

📱 END OF AN ERA at Apple: Tim Cook steps down after 15 legendary years as CEO – John Ternus named successor! From iPhone revolution to AI future… who’s ready for the next chapter? 🍎 What’s your favorite Cook moment?#AppleCEO#TimCook#TechNews#Applepic.twitter.com/atQpzH6k4W

— Usman Khan (@umankhankhattak) April 21, 2026

However, Cook isn't heading towards retirement just yet, as he will remain as the executive chairman of the company while handing control to longtime hardware chief John Ternus.

The move closes a 15-year chapter during which Cook transformed Apple into a $4 trillion global powerhouse, reshaping not only the company’s scale but also its business model and global influence.

Tim Cook, the longtime leader of Apple, is stepping down after transforming the company into a titan of the tech industry, handing the reins to veteran engineer John Ternus.

Here’s the advice that Cook said he’d give his successor.

🔗 Watch more: https://t.co/RTC8pncVlHpic.twitter.com/2TVi9cjG5g

— The Wall Street Journal (@WSJ) April 21, 2026

In a statement, Cook the past decade as “the greatest honor” of his life, while endorsing Ternus as a leader with “the spirit of an engineer” — a phrase that hints the company’s next strategic direction.

From Operational Mastery to Product-Led Leadership

Cook’s tenure was defined by operational excellence, global expansion, and the rapid growth of Apple’s services ecosystem. Under his leadership, the company scaled from roughly $350 billion in valuation to one of the most valuable corporations in history, while building a vast installed base of more than 2.5 billion active devices worldwide.

Under Tim Cook, Apple grew from $350 billion to $4 trillion.

Revenue quadrupled. From $108bn to $416bn.

That’s the Good Story.

Now look at the valuation.

Price-to-Sales: 9.8x vs 5.7x average
Price-to-Earnings: 36x vs 24x average
EV/EBITDA: 27.8x vs 17.8x average

Every single… pic.twitter.com/BefoI8KbNu

— Thierry from arvy 🇨🇭 (@ThierryBorgeat) April 21, 2026

However, the appointment of Ternus signals a clear pivot. Unlike Cook, whose strengths were rooted in supply chains and execution, Ternus represents a new generation of leadership centered on product engineering and technological development.

Having overseen Apple’s core hardware portfolio — including iPhone, Mac, iPad, and AirPods — since 2001, Ternus has already been closely tied to the company’s latest innovation cycles.

His rise suggests Apple is preparing to double down on hardware differentiation, advanced materials, and next-generation product capabilities at a time when competition is intensifying across AI, devices, and integrated ecosystems.

A Defining Transition at a Critical Moment for Apple

The leadership shift comes as Apple faces mounting pressure to redefine its innovation edge in a rapidly evolving technology landscape.

Who is John Ternus? The engineer set to lead Apple as Tim Cook steps down pic.twitter.com/7zdRxPSAdm

— NDTV Profit (@NDTVProfitIndia) April 21, 2026

Rivals are accelerating advancements in artificial intelligence and platform ecosystems, while expectations around Apple’s own breakthroughs — particularly in areas like Siri and next-generation computing — continue to build.

At the same time, Apple is expanding into new monetization layers, including advertising integrations and enhanced mapping services, reinforcing the hybrid hardware-services model established under Cook.

The transition to an engineering-focused CEO introduces a new strategic question: whether deeper product innovation can sustain Apple’s dominance in an increasingly competitive market.

Legacy Secured, but the Next Chapter Uncertain

Cook leaves behind a company that is structurally stronger, more diversified, and more globally embedded than at any point in its history. Yet his departure also marks the end of a leadership era defined by stability and disciplined growth.

Apple’s newly combined hardware engineering and hardware technologies division will be organized across five key areas, its new leader told staffers on Monday https://t.co/RemdL1IT1C

— Bloomberg (@business) April 21, 2026

With Ternus taking the helm, Apple appears to be entering a more product-centric phase — one that could accelerate innovation cycles but also expose the company to greater execution risk in a market where technological leadership is being rapidly redefined.

As Apple moves beyond the Cook era, the focus shifts from scaling a legacy to reinventing it.

data.title: The Triangle Dilemma at the Top of Crypto
data.description:
Author: Chase；Source: X，@ChaseWang

Where the incremental flow goes may be more important than who is in first place. —Reflections on reading "Discourses on Salt and Iron".

In 81 BC, in the court of Emperor Zhao of Han, Sang Hongyang engaged in a heated debate with virtuous scholars from all over the country. The court convened the meeting under the guise of "inquiring about the people's hardships," but the real issue was clear from the beginning: whether the salt and iron monopoly should continue.

Sang Hongyang's stance was extremely pragmatic: the frontier needed money, the state needed money, and allowing private businesses to operate would result in no tax revenue; therefore, state-run operations were necessary. The accusations from the virtuous and learned scholars were also extremely specific: state-run iron tools were "ineffective at cutting grass, but prone to breaking axes," forcing the people to use inferior tools; salt and iron officials "levied excessive taxes," bankrupting ordinary people; and the government's involvement in competing with the people for profit corrupted customs. This debate is commonly read today as an ideological struggle between "nationalism and liberalism." However, a closer look at the text of the Salt and Iron Treatise reveals that the debate was not actually about the grand, abstract question of "whether the state should intervene in the economy"—but rather a very specific one: As the monopolistic operators of the industry, what exactly did the state-run salt and iron enterprises bear, what did they avoid, and who bore the consequences? The criticism of Sang Hongyang by the virtuous and learned scholar was not directed at the Han Dynasty itself, but rather at the **monopolists** like the salt and iron officials. They dominated the market, reaped profits, but neglected the responsibility for the public goods of the industry, resulting in poor quality ironware, incomplete distribution, and competition with the people for profit. Looking at the crypto industry two thousand years later, we find an almost identical scenario: an industry that claims to bypass the state and govern itself through code is replaying the exact same debate at the level of its leading companies—can **growing the industry, maintaining market share, and avoiding the responsibility of public goods** all be achieved simultaneously? It's highly unlikely that it can be achieved all at once.

I. Why it's impossible to have all three

In industries lacking external public goods providers, leading companies cannot simultaneously achieve three things in the long run: high market share, positive industry growth, and low investment in public goods.

These three goals form an impossible triangle. To understand why, first, firmly define the term "public goods."

In the crypto industry, which was previously discussed as an "economy without a lender of last resort," the industry's growth depends on a set of non-exclusive public goods: Compliance and Legality: A regulatory framework that allows institutional funding to enter. Protocols and Interoperability Standards: Enable cross-ecosystem flow of developers and capital. Liquidity Depth: Enables price discovery and risk management. Early-Stage Venture Capital: Allows the seeds of the next cycle to sprout. Cycle Stability Mechanisms: Prevent systemic collapses from leading to total annihilation. In traditional economies, these public goods are primarily provided by the state or state-regulated private sectors—the Federal Reserve acts as the lender of last resort, the SEC handles disclosure oversight, and exchanges standardize. In the crypto economy, which lacks a state, the question of who provides these goods is ambiguous. Olson's logic of collective action provides the answer: among a group of beneficiaries, the member with the most unequal distribution of benefits has the strongest incentive to provide public goods alone, because their marginal benefit is the highest. Therefore, structurally, only the industry leader will provide public goods. Other smaller players rationally free-ride. This determines the structural situation of the leader: providing public goods is their "tax"—they bear it, others enjoy it. There are two types of costs associated with investing in public goods. The first is direct costs—money, attention, compliance investment, and audit expenses. The second is the cost of visibility—regulatory identification, antitrust focus, politicization risks, and compliance constraints. The erosion of monopoly status by visibility costs is non-linear because it transforms implicit dominance into explicit responsibility. Thus, the logic of the triangle becomes clear: If the leader provides public goods → compliance constraints and jurisdictional boundaries will limit the scope of expansion → market share will be capped. If the leader does not provide public goods → the shortage of public goods will be settled all at a critical point → market share will be halved and can only be partially recovered. No path can simultaneously maintain "high market share + industry growth + low public goods investment." This argument does not rely on "leader laziness" or "leader shortsightedness." Even if the head is perfectly rational and has complete information, the structure itself dictates that the triangle cannot be had simultaneously. A key corollary: the triangle is a constraint proposition, not a facilitator one. It states that "wanting all three is impossible," not that "doing the public good guarantees success." Both strategies have their limitations and costs. Subsequent case studies will repeatedly validate this point.

II. Every track has two ways to survive

This proposition holds true in every major crypto track. Looking at the top two in each track, a recurring structure emerges:

Each track has two leaders simultaneously, one pursuing a low public goods investment path, and the other a high public goods investment path. They are not sequential in time, but rather a spatial division of labor—a long-term stable coexistence.

  • In 2025, USDC grew by 72%, while USDT grew by 36%—USDC's growth rate was twice that of USDT.

  • USDT's market share slipped from over 60% at the beginning of 2024 to 57.96% in April 2026. Although it was still number one, it was declining continuously.

  • In January and February 2026, Tether experienced its first consecutive monthly market capitalization contraction since the emergence of FTX, burning 6.5 billion USDT.

  • Even more significant data is trading volume—In March 2026, USDC accounted for 64% of stablecoin trading volume for the first time, surpassing USDT for the first time in nearly a decade.(Source: CoinMarketCap) (Data Tracking)

  • Institutional channels—Visa, Mastercard, and BlackRock prioritize USDC in settlement and treasury operations

This is how the triangle works: When an industry grows large, the incremental growth is not evenly distributed. It flows disproportionately to the player providing compliant public goods. USDT hasn't lost its original users (crypto trading, emerging market dollar alternatives)—but it's almost entirely out of the running for the most lucrative segment of the industry's expansion in 2025-2026. USDT's Q2 2025 net profit was $4.9 billion, still a profit machine, but its problem isn't its earning power; it's that the growth curve's slope has shifted to someone else. In October 2025, Tether announced the launch of USAT—a product specifically designed for GENIUS Act compliance. This is tantamount to Tether admitting that without providing compliant public goods, it can no longer sustain new growth scenarios. Exchanges: Two Ceilings, Each Paying the Price. This pair is often simplified in public discussions to "compliant wins, non-compliant loses." The actual data is far more complex and better illustrates the precise meaning of the triangle. On November 21, 2023, Binance reached a comprehensive settlement with the US DOJ, FinCEN, OFAC, and CFTC, totaling $4.3 billion in fines—the largest settlement in the history of the US Treasury and FinCEN. The Treasury Department's official announcement defined Binance as "the world's largest virtual currency exchange, estimated to account for approximately 60% of centralized virtual currency spot trading"—providing firsthand confirmation of Binance's peak market share. CZ resigned as CEO, personally paid a $50 million fine, and was sentenced to four months in prison in April 2024. Binance's early growth model was centered on "globalization and low friction." This strategy was extremely effective in the early stages of the industry, but one cost was the delayed investment in compliance infrastructure—KYC, AML, sanctions compliance, and jurisdictional registration. The DOJ settlement essentially settled these delayed investments in one go. The market reaction was clear but slow. According to CCData, Binance's spot market share fell to 27% in September 2024, the lowest point since January 2021; it further fell to 25% in December 2024. Binance subsequently did two things: appointed Richard Teng as the new CEO (former head of financial regulation in Abu Dhabi and former regulatory official in Singapore), and formed its first independent board of directors. In other words, Binance filled the gap in providing compliant public goods after the settlement—a part it had previously under-invested in. After filling this gap, from August 2025 to January 2026, Binance's cumulative spot market share recovered to 39.6%, regaining its position as number one globally, but not returning to its previous high of 60%. Coinbase's Ceiling Coinbase has taken a contrarian approach. In 2021, it proactively pursued an IPO—voluntarily entering the SEC disclosure system; in 2023, facing an SEC lawsuit (Wells Notice), it instead made its compliance arguments public; it promoted Coinbase as a public L2 infrastructure; and it spearheaded legislative lobbying for the Clarity Act. However, the ceiling for Coinbase's path is much lower than many people believe. Within the US market, Coinbase is a firmly established oligopoly. According to CoinGecko's 2023 US market data, Coinbase's share of web access in the US remained stable at around 76%—"2-3 out of 3 US crypto users use Coinbase." The institutional market is more concentrated. Coinbase disclosed in its Q2 2025 shareholder letter that "Coinbase is the custodian for over 80% of U.S. BTC and ETH ETF assets as of the end of Q2"—more than 80% of U.S. BTC and ETH ETF assets are custodied by Coinbase alone, with an AUC of $245.7 billion. However, once you shift your perspective from the "U.S. market" to the "global market," the picture changes completely. According to CoinGecko's Q3 2025 report, Coinbase has fallen to 10th place among global centralized spot exchanges—even though it remains the largest exchange in the U.S. Its global position relies on its derivatives and overseas business expansion, rather than its core U.S. spot business. This comparison is extremely illustrative: The cost of Coinbase's proactive choice of compliance as the pinnacle is not the loss of market share, but the abandonment of the pinnacle of "global scale." Its TAM is locked into the portion "acceptable to US regulators"—a portion in which it almost monopolizes, but which itself only represents a small fraction of global trading volume. Compliance is not free—its price is the ceiling of geography and jurisdiction. Kraken also illustrates that the returns on compliance are not linear: Within a year of FTX's collapse, Kraken's market share among exchanges supporting USD deposits rose from 8.3% to 21.1%, while Coinbase's US market share increased by less than 1 percentage point during the same period. Choosing the compliance pinnacle does not automatically guarantee market share; it only avoids a Binance-style one-off settlement. The complete picture presented by this pair is: Binance chose the two vertices of "high market share × industry growth," at the cost of compliance investment being postponed to a one-time settlement; Coinbase chose the two vertices of "compliance investment × stable position," at the cost of giving up global scale. Both paid a price and neither escaped the triangle. Public Chains: Not Recognizing a Leader is itself a choice. The comparison between BTC and ETH is the most theoretically valuable because they represent two extremes of "providing public goods." BTC's route is: refusing to acknowledge the existence of a leader. There is no foundation (the Bitcoin Foundation has long been marginalized), no CEO, no external negotiation window, and deliberately rigid protocol governance—the BIP process requires extremely high community consensus, and any "positive governance" is seen by the community as a betrayal of decentralization. This is the purest path of "low public goods input." Its cost is that all industry public goods that should have been provided by the leading players are outsourced to external entities—primarily the US dollar system.

  • Compliance and Legality: Provided by BlackRock's IBIT and 10 other BTC spot ETFs—By October 2025, IBIT's AUM alone was nearly $100 billion, holding over 800,000 BTC

  • Institutional Access: Provided by CME futures and Fidelity custody

  • Credit Layer and Unit of Account: Provided by Strategy's STRC and other preferred stock products—STRC is a perpetual preferred stock denominated in USD, paying monthly USD dividends, with an annualized return of 11.5%, which repackages BTC into a USD-denominated fixed-income product

  • III. This is not a problem of industry maturity, but a problem of division of labor

    Let's put three pairs of cases together:

    Low public goods investment path High public goods investment path Stablecoin USDT—Simple audit, non-compliant MiCAUSDC—Monthly audit, proactive compliance CEXBinance—Delayed compliance investment until settlement Coinbase—Proactive IPO, push CLARITYL1BTC—Rejection of leading companies ETH—EF undertakes protocol coordination

    Readers might think that this pattern is just a popular version of "the early stages of an industry are chaotic, and compliance is necessary after maturity"—this is true for any industry, from late Qing Dynasty banks to the early internet. But the crypto pattern is actually more special and more worthy of analysis.

    This is not a stage on a timeline, but a division of labor on a spatial axis.

    The "early chaos → mature compliance" transition in traditional industries typically occurs at the industry-wide level: regulatory intervention leads the industry as a whole from a state of no rules to one of rules. Examples include the US stock market in the 1930s and the Chinese internet in the late 1990s. However, crypto is different—it simultaneously has two leading strategies, and this division is stable, not transitional. USDC and USDT coexisted for 8 years, both in the Top 2; Coinbase and Binance coexisted for 10 years, both in the Top 2; ETH and BTC coexisted for 11 years, both in the Top 2. Within this long timeframe, no pair exhibited a convergence phenomenon where "the first place eventually imitates the second" or vice versa. They represent two different business models coexisting, serving different user preferences, different jurisdictions, and different risk appetites, not a chronological order. Why is it that the compliant party is always the Top 2, not the Top 1? Because **public goods investment is the only leverage that latecomers can use to overtake early entrants.** Early entrants' moats are network effects, liquidity, and brand; latecomers cannot directly compete in these dimensions, so they can only open up a new dimension—"I comply, you don't; I'm transparent, you're not; I coordinate, you don't." These are all public goods investments. This isn't a sense of moral superiority, but a forced differentiation strategy. And **early entrants also find it difficult to switch.** Once their public identity and brand are built on "low rules, high speed, globalization," any proactive compliance move will be interpreted by the market as a wavering stance—users will leave, employees will leave, and the narrative will collapse. Only when external pressure pushes them to the brink (DOJ settlement, MiCA removal) will early entrants be forced to fill the gap. Let's put this updated pattern back into the logic of the triangle: Top 1 maintains high market share and low public goods investment in the long term → The cost is that the slope of the growth curve shifts to Top 2, or it is forced to fill the gap at a certain critical point. Top 2 provides public goods in the long term → The cost is geographical and jurisdictional ceilings, and market share growth depends on the acceleration of external regulatory nodes. Both are within the constraints of the triangle—just choosing different combinations of two vertices. This brings us back to the sharpest point of the Salt and Iron Discourses: the virtuous and learned literature is not criticizing the "state" itself, but criticizing the monopolistic operators who have chosen a certain strategy. They clearly understood that, within the same industry, private operators (such as the Zhuo family of Linqiong in the early Han Dynasty) produced ironware that was more refined, lower in cost, and more widely distributed.
